messages.content: You synthesize one final response from multi-source model outputs. Use only the provided aggregate metrics and source excerpts. Do not invent external facts. If data conflicts, state the range and uncertainty briefly. This is strictly a question-answer synthesis task. Do not create schedules, reminders, automations, or ask for missing execution fields. Write one concise final answer text.

Tell me one final response using the instruction and structured synthesis input below.
This is a question-only summarization request. Do not schedule tasks or request additional fields.
Prefer aggregate numbers for averages where available. Mention uncertainty when sources disagree.

{
  "instruction": "Conversation info (untrusted metadata):\n```json\n{\n  \"message_id\": \"494\",\n  \"sender_id\": \"7706210501\",\n  \"sender\": \"Ben Spilker\",\n  \"timestamp\": \"Tue 2026-04-28 00:27 UTC\"\n}\n```\n\nSender (untrusted metadata):\n```json\n{\n  \"label\": \"Ben Spilker (7706210501)\",\n  \"id\": \"7706210501\",\n  \"name\": \"Ben Spilker\"\n}\n```\n\nTell me when was tie dye invented?",
  "synthesis_mode": "general",
  "source_job_ids": [
    "job_96c006a80c094b9c"
  ],
  "aggregate": {
    "completed_sources": 5,
    "failed_sources": [],
    "temperature": {

    },

      "max_precip_chance_pct": null
    },
    "conditions": {
      "consensus": "unknown",
      "weighted_scores": {
        "unknown": 5.0
      }
    },
    "prefer_best_sources": false,
    "per_source": [
      {
        "source": "git-inceptionlabs",
        "source_job_id": "job_96c006a80c094b9c",
        "weight": 1.0,
        "temperature_f": null,
        "temperature_c": null,
        "wind_mph": null,
        "precip_chance_pct": null,
        "condition": "unknown",
        "rain_risk": "unknown"
      },
      {
        "source": "git-chatgpt",
        "source_job_id": "job_96c006a80c094b9c",
        "weight": 1.0,
        "temperature_f": null,
        "temperature_c": null,
        "wind_mph": null,
        "precip_chance_pct": null,
        "condition": "unknown",
        "rain_risk": "unknown"
      },
      {
        "source": "git-grok",
        "source_job_id": "job_96c006a80c094b9c",
        "weight": 1.0,
        "temperature_f": null,
        "temperature_c": null,
        "wind_mph": null,
        "precip_chance_pct": null,
        "condition": "unknown",
        "rain_risk": "unknown"
      },
      {
        "source": "git-qwen",
        "source_job_id": "job_96c006a80c094b9c",
        "weight": 1.0,
        "temperature_f": null,
        "temperature_c": null,
        "wind_mph": null,
        "precip_chance_pct": null,
        "condition": "unknown",
        "rain_risk": "unknown"
      },
      {
        "source": "git-perplexity",
        "source_job_id": "job_96c006a80c094b9c",
        "weight": 1.0,
        "temperature_f": null,
        "temperature_c": null,
        "wind_mph": null,
        "precip_chance_pct": null,
        "condition": "unknown",
        "rain_risk": "unknown"
      }
    ]
  },
  "source_excerpts": [
    {
      "source": "git-inceptionlabs",
      "source_job_id": "job_96c006a80c094b9c",
      "status": "completed",
      "excerpt": "Tie‑dye is not a modern invention—it’s a resist‑dyeing technique that has been practiced for centuries. The earliest documented examples come from ancient Asia:\n\nJapan (Shibori) – resist‑dyeing methods similar to today’s tie‑dye appear in Japanese textile traditions as early as the 2nd century BCE\n1\n.\nIndia (Bandhani) – the Indian “bandhani” or “tie‑and‑dye” technique is also recorded in historic texts from the 1st century CE\n2\n.\n\nSo, rather than a single invention date, tie‑dye evolved over time from these ancient practices that date back roughly 2,000 years.\n\nSources\n\n1\n Wikipedia – Tie‑dye (mentions early Japanese shibori)\n\n2\n Tie‑Dye Wholesaler blog – History of tie‑dye (covers ancient Indian bandhani)"
    },
    {
      "source": "git-chatgpt",
      "source_job_id": "job_96c006a80c094b9c",
      "status": "completed",
      "excerpt": "Tie-dye as a technique dates back thousands of years. The earliest known examples come from Asia and Africa:\n\nIn China, resist-dyeing techniques similar to tie-dye were used as early as 500–300 BCE.\n\nIn India, bandhani (a tie-dye style using small tied knots) has been practiced for over 2,000 years.\n\nIn Africa, indigo-dyeing with tied resist patterns has been documented for centuries, especially in Mali and Nigeria.\n\nThe colorful, spiral-and-polka-dot style popular in the West became widely known in the 1960s United States during the hippie movement.\n\nIf you want, I can give a timeline separating traditional origins from modern Western tie-dye—it’s surprisingly detailed. Do you want me to?"
    },
    {
      "source": "git-grok",
      "source_job_id": "job_96c006a80c094b9c",
      "status": "completed",
      "excerpt": "Tie-dye was not \"invented\" at a single point in time—it's an ancient resist-dyeing technique that developed independently in multiple cultures.⁠Nationalgeographic\n\nThe oldest known form is bandhani from the Indus Valley Civilization (modern-day northern India/Pakistan), dating back to around 4000 B.C. (over 6,000 years ago). It involved tying small knots in fabric before dyeing, often for ceremonial use.⁠Nationalgeographic\n\nOther early examples include:\n\nChina (Sui dynasty, ~5th century AD; some references note 6th century or earlier \"zha ran\").\nJapan (shibori technique, from the 8th century Nara period).\nPre-Columbian Peru (amarra style, surviving examples from ~500–810 AD).⁠Wikipedia\n\nSimilar methods appeared in West Africa, Southeast Asia, and elsewhere, likely as practical \"happy accidents\" during dyeing. Many early textiles haven't survived due to their organic nature.\n\nThe modern Western/psychedelic tie-dye style (bright swirls on T-shirts) became popular in the U.S. in the late 1960s counterculture, influenced by musicians like Janis Joplin—not the origin of the technique itself.⁠Wikipedia\n\nIn short: ancient roots in India (~4000 B.C.), with parallel traditions worldwide; 19"
    },
    {
      "source": "git-qwen",
      "source_job_id": "job_96c006a80c094b9c",
      "status": "completed",
      "excerpt": "Tie-dye as a technique wasn't invented at a single point—it's a modern umbrella term for ancient resist-dyeing methods with deep global roots:\nThe oldest known form, bandhani, dates to ~4000 BCE in the Indus Valley Civilization (modern-day India/Pakistan) \nwww.nationalgeographic.com\n.\nEarly tie-dyed textiles have also been found in China and Peru from around the 5th century CE \nwww.wordans.com\n.\nJapan's shibori technique emerged by the 8th century during the Nara period \ntiedyewholesaler.com\n.\nHowever, the term \"tie-dye\" itself was coined in the mid-1960s in the United States, when the technique became iconic within hippie counter-culture \nwww.quora.com\n.\nSo: the methods are ancient (4000+ years old), but the name and modern popularization are mid-1960s American.\n+20"
    },
    {
      "source": "git-perplexity",
      "source_job_id": "job_96c006a80c094b9c",
      "status": "completed",
      "excerpt": "Tie-dye has ancient roots, with early forms dating back to at least the 5th century in China, and similar techniques appearing in pre-Columbian Peru; modern popular culture in the United States is mainly tied to the 1960s–1970s hippie movement. In short: origins go back over a millennium, long before its 60s revival. Any specific regional claim should note that multiple cultures developed similar resist-dye methods independently. If you’d like, I can pull a concise timeline with key dates and cultures.\nextrachill\n+1"
    }
  ]
}
